data.title: Starbucks: An Offensive Reset Is Brewing At Starbucks - Buy
data.description:
Introduction

For much of the past year, Starbucks (SBUX) has been described in uninspiring terms. Margin pressure. Higher labor costs. Store closures. But that framing misses what is really happening. Under Brian Niccol, Starbucks is not settling for a modest recovery. The company is aiming for the fences. Investment in service quality is being prioritized to drive sales growth, with margins expected to follow. Throughput is becoming the central operating priority to enhance execution in peak hours. In store, mobile, drive through, and delivery are being reworked to function as one part of an ecosystem rather than competing channels. This is not a turnaround built on cost cutting. It is a deliberate bet that better service and faster order flow can drive meaningful comparable sales growth, improve store level economics, and restore operating profit margins. The 4% YoY increase in Q1 FY26 comparable store sales offers an early indication that service enhancements are resonating with customers and that Niccol’s strategy is starting to harness the power of the Starbucks brand.

Investment Thesis

My investment thesis for Starbucks rests on three core pillars:

  1. A service-led reset sits at the core of the Back to Starbucks strategy and is the company’s primary lever to drive comparable sales growth. Early signs indicate that this focus on service is beginning to lift like-for-like sales.
  2. At the Analyst Day, Starbucks highlighted that by FY28 it aims to reach a run rate of adding 2,000 new stores per year, of which roughly 400 are expected to be in the U.S. For a company that already operates one of the largest restaurant chains in the world with more than 41,000 stores globally, this cadence of new store openings underscores the depth of the organic growth opportunity still ahead. Accordingly, new store additions should remain a material contributor to top-line growth over the medium term.
  3. The joint venture with Boyu Capital in China both derisks Starbucks’ operations and accelerates growth in one of its most important international markets. Partnering with a local firm provides deeper on-the-ground expertise and enables a faster and more disciplined expansion into tier three, four, and five cities, an approach Starbucks relies on in many of its international markets.

A Service Reset

Back to Starbucks is a company-wide reset designed to refocus the business on service quality and the in-store experience to drive growth. The strategy is centered on reclaiming the “third place” through an enhanced customer experience, a simplified yet innovative menu, and store uplifts aimed at driving repeat customer visits. This entails investing inside the coffee house through expanded staffing, next-generation espresso equipment, and order sequencing technology to support better execution during peak periods. This approach appears to be working, as it is generating higher transaction volumes among both Rewards and non-Rewards customers for the first time in four years.

Comparable Store Sales Move Back into Growth

Starbucks delivered Q1 FY26 results that showed a better-than-expected recovery in sales. The company achieved a top line of $9.9 billion, which was 2.7% ahead of consensus and 6% above the same period last year. More importantly, this result also exceeded the highest sales estimate from Wall Street of $9.79 billion. There was broad-based strength from a segment perspective. North America achieved a 4% YoY uplift in sales and accounted for 73% of total revenue, while international markets grew by 10% YoY and represented 21% of total sales.

The driver of this solid top line performance was growth in comparable store sales. At the group level, Starbucks posted a 4% increase in comparable store sales. The main driver of this increase was a 3% rise in transactions, which is the preferred growth metric to see in the context of a turnaround. This improvement also reflects a relatively balanced contribution from the two main reporting segments, with North America achieving a 4% increase in comparable store sales and international markets delivering a 5% increase. Overall, this performance is very solid and represents an early indication that the turnaround is starting to materialize. Figure 1 shows that this is the first meaningful recovery in comparable store sales over the past 8 quarters.

Figure 1: Comparable Store Sales by Quarter

Adjusted Operating Profit Margin Remains Under Pressure

The disappointing aspect of Q1 FY26 was that the compression in the adjusted operating profit margin remained elevated. The adjusted operating profit margin compressed by 180 basis points YoY to 10.1% in Q1 FY26. This was driven by a 480 basis point compression in the operating profit margin in North America. Most of the margin pressure in North America emanates from investments to support the Back to Starbucks strategy and higher coffee prices due to tariffs. This likely acted as a sour point for investors, as it suggests that margin pressure may persist for a couple of quarters as the Back to Starbucks investments continue to ramp up. It is important to note that tariffs on coffee were lifted in November 2025 and thus should not play a major role in margin compression throughout FY26.

Figure 2 shows the declining trend in trailing twelve months (TTM) operating profit margin, which now stands at 9.5%. A successful turnaround at Starbucks will be reflected in both sustained comparable store sales growth and a recovery in the adjusted operating profit margin. While Q1 FY26 results suggest that Niccol and his team still have work ahead to restore profitability, the current margin pressure should be viewed in the context of deliberate investment in service quality. Given that the strategy has been to fix service in order to grow sales, investors may need to wait until the company begins to lap Back to Starbucks investments before material improvements in operating profit margins become visible.

Figure 2: TTM Adjusted Operating Profit

Dividends Are Secure While Buyback Resumption May Take Time

There is concern among some investors that Starbucks will likely have to cut its dividend, an action that would likely have significant ramifications for the stock price. Figure 3 shows how the FCF-generating ability of Starbucks has declined over the last ten quarters. The early decline in FCF led to the company’s decision to suspend share buybacks. More recently, TTM FCF has fallen below TTM dividends, resulting in a $0.5 billion annual funding gap. It is this gap that is causing many to be concerned about a potential dividend cut, a conclusion that is rather hasty in my opinion.

Figure 3: TTM FCF and Cash Returns to Shareholders

First, given that the turnaround in sales is starting to take place, we should expect an inflection in operating profits sometime in FY26, most likely in Q3 FY26. This suggests that pressure on FCF has likely reached its peak and that we are approaching a period where FCF should begin to recover. Second, Starbucks has $3.4 billion in cash, which should be sufficient to support any dividend funding gap in FY26. Third, Starbucks is expected to conclude the sale of 60% of its China operations to Boyu in Spring 2026, which should generate approximately $2.4 billion in cash proceeds (calculated by author based on 60% of the announced deal value of $4 billion and excludes any potential tax impacts). Accordingly, while a modest funding gap between FCF and dividends is visible on a TTM basis in the near term, management is not under pressure to reduce the dividend to cover this gap.

Figure 4: Cash Flow Bridge

Outlook: Management Reinstates FY26 Guidance and Sets FY28 Targets

Starbucks reinstated its guidance for FY26. There are several takeaways from this guidance. First, management expects comparable store sales to be positive in FY26 and to act as the primary driver of top-line growth. However, the 3% guide appears modest compared to the 4% rate achieved in Q1 FY26. This guide likely factors in tougher comparisons in the second half of the year, as Q4 FY25 was already positive. Second, on a full year basis, profitability is expected to improve both in terms of operating profit margin and EPS. The guide for operating profit margin exceeding 10% is above the 9.9% achieved in FY25. In addition, even the lower end of the EPS guide of $2.15 is slightly ahead of FY25 adjusted EPS of $2.13. Given the profit contraction witnessed in Q1 FY26, the guide implies a recovery in profitability in the second half of FY26.

Figure 5: FY26 Guide

In the Analyst Day, management also provided a framework on how they expect the company’s performance to look in FY28 as part of a three-year forward guide. First, in terms of sales growth, they expect the growth rate to exceed 5%, driven by a 3% contribution from comparable store sales and an additional 2% to 3% from new store openings. The company plans to open more than 2,000 stores in FY28, of which approximately 400 are U.S. company-operated stores. From a profitability perspective, the guide calls for an operating profit margin of 13.5% to 15%. That is well ahead of the approximately 10% expected in FY26 but below the 16.1% achieved in FY23. For me, this guide feels somewhat conservative given the extent of the reset that is taking shape. It may reflect management’s effort to temper Street expectations around the pace of the turnaround, while still leaving meaningful upside for a bull case scenario.

Figure 6: FY28 Framework

Valuation

Given that Starbucks is in the midst of a turnaround, I prefer to look at EV/Sales as the primary valuation metric. The current operating profit margin remains well below both its historical level and management’s target for FY28, which makes earnings multiples noisy. Starbucks currently trades at a forward EV/Sales multiple of 3.4x, which is approximately 12% below its five-year average. While this suggests the stock is not expensive relative to its own history, it would be difficult to argue that valuation is cheap. The market appears to be pricing in a relatively high probability of a successful turnaround. Accordingly, any meaningful rally in the stock price is likely to require sales growth above 5% and operating profit margin gains ahead of expectation to justify a further multiple re-rating.

Key Risks

Though Starbucks does not have a single direct competitor of comparable scale, it operates in a highly competitive industry with many direct and indirect rivals. In addition, the company is exposed to macroeconomic risks that can affect consumer spending. During periods of pressure on discretionary spending, premium beverage purchases are often among the first areas where consumers cut back. Consumer preferences can also shift quickly, and Starbucks may not always be able to adapt its offerings at the same pace. Health and wellness trends represent another risk, particularly for demand related to sugary beverages. Coffee is a key imported input for Starbucks. Thus, volatility in coffee prices or the imposition of tariffs can pressure margins.

Final Thoughts

The Q1 FY26 results provided investors with an early glimpse that the turnaround is starting to take shape, marked by a 4% increase in comparable store sales. This represents the first step toward reigniting sales growth. The next phase will be demonstrating that sustained improvement in comparable store sales can translate into a recovery in operating profit margins. While management reinstated guidance for FY26, the outlook appears conservative with 3% growth in comparable sales. I believe the impact of the Back to Starbucks strategy has the potential to compound over time, supporting stronger gains in comparable store sales. As a result, I view management’s guidance as a base case, with a high probability of delivering comparable sales growth in the 3% to 5% range in FY26. While the stock is not cheap, it offers meaningful upside if execution continues to improve. I assign a Buy rating to the stock.

data.title: S&P Global: Standardizing Private Markets Unlocks Spin-Off Value
data.description:
S&P Global (SPGI) stock is a Strong Buy as per my assessment. The reason behind the rating is S&P Global’s engineered shift from an information provider to the structural interoperability layer of the $21 trillion private markets ecosystem. The integration of With Intelligence’s allocator data with iLEVEL’s workflow tools backs S&P Global to standardize private asset transparency. This integration is creating a moat comparable to its public ratings advantage. Risks exist from the Agentic erosion of seat-based revenue in Market Intelligence and potential regulatory caps on pricing power. However, S&P Global’s shift toward 95% proprietary revenue and the possible value unlock from the Mobility spin-off build a defensive growth profile that outweighs cyclical issuance revenue volatility.

The Bull Case: The Standardization Alpha in Private Markets

The fundamental factor that I see capable of driving up S&P Global’s stock price (over the long term) is the growth of private markets and S&P Global’s engineered capability to force a structural standardization of private market infrastructure. Simply, I am highly confident that S&P Global can replicate its public market dominance (GICS, Ratings) within the $21 trillion private asset ecosystem. Although the market efficiency integrates the With Intelligence acquisition and the Cambridge Associates/Mercer partnerships as growth drivers. However, my thesis’s focus/concentration is on the deeper valuation potency that lies in S&P Global’s move to become the interoperability layer for the private capital stack.

As of now, the private market suffers from a transparency gap. The gap is a lack of standardized data for benchmarking, valuation, and credit assessment. Now, through integrating With Intelligence’s allocator-sourced data (30,000+ LPs/GPs) with iLEVEL’s portfolio monitoring (700+ clients tracking 16 billion+ data points) and S&P’s proprietary credit methodologies, S&P Global can create a self-reinforcing network effect. This effect builds a case where General Partners [GPs] must adopt S&P’s reporting standards to satisfy Limited Partners [LPs] who utilize iLEVEL and With Intelligence for allocation decisions. This is very different from a simple data sales model. It is a market structure play—if S&P Global forms the universal taxonomy for private credit and equity performance (similar to how it standardized public sectors), it will secure a big moat.

From the financial side, this standardization alpha is a shift in the quality of Market Intelligence [MI] and ratings revenue. Logically, as private credit matures and inevitably counters a default cycle, the demand for S&P’s independent Credit estimates and surveillance will transition from discretionary to regulatory/fiduciary necessities. There is a $9 trillion asset-based financing opportunity by 2029 and a target of 6%-9% growth for Ratings. However, if S&P Global becomes the de facto clearinghouse for private market data interoperability, the pricing power in Market Intelligence (currently 32.5% adjusted margin) and Ratings (63% margin) can decouple from issuance volumes. This is the structural shift I am pointing to, that may lead to a large expansion in the PE multiple. The shift can move SPGI stock from a cyclical financial services valuation toward a high-moat information utility valuation and may derive big price returns well above the projected mid-teens EPS growth.

The Bear Case: The Agentic Erosion of the Seat-Based Premium

On the flip side, there is forward risk to S&P Global's stock price, and it is the Agentic Erosion of the high-margin seat-based workstation model within Market Intelligence. The risk is coming from the fast adoption of autonomous AI agents by institutional clients. I do not consider gen AI as a productivity tailwind for S&P Global (through Kensho, ChatIQ, and Spark Assist), for me, the unpriced risk is the deflationary pressure on the unit economics of information consumption.

To clarify more, Market Intelligence derives ~36% of total revenue (TTM Q3) with a heavy dependence on Desktop revenue and Data & Advisory. The bull case depends on human analysts using tools like Capital IQ Pro more sharply. However, the buy-side and sell-side clients are aggressively building internal Agentic AI workflows (autonomous software agents that retrieve specific data points via API without human intervention). For instance, as per Saugata Saha (Chief Enterprise Data Officer) clients want data independent of organizational structure and machine-to-machine. Although S&P is shifting to LLM-ready APIs, the unit economics of an API call are logically different from a recurring high-priced per-seat license.

As an example, if a large investment bank replaces 500 junior analyst seats (say $20k+/year each) with a single enterprise API feed accessed by internal agents, the revenue capture for S&P Global can be compressed largely. This can happen even if data volume increases. S&P admits that ~12% of Market Intelligence revenue is undifferentiated (public filings, transcripts). In an agentic world, this undifferentiated data becomes a commodity with near-zero value. Agents can scrape and synthesize the data from free sources instantly. Hence, S&P’s defense is its 95% proprietary data moat, but the delivery mechanism is the profit driver.

Moreover, a shift from high-margin visual interfaces [GUI] to lower-margin data feeds (headless consumption) can distort the 50-75 bps annual margin expansion target. At last, if the Terminal model dies as machines don't need user interfaces, Market Intelligence's organic growth can structurally stall below the 6%-8% target, and this can force a de-rating of SPGI stock as Wall Street realizes the high-margin seat model is a legacy asset in an AI-operated financial ecosystem.

SPGI Stock Valuation Through The Post-Spin Quality Re-Rating Model Marks $869 by 2029

I value SPGI stock on a post spin quality basis by isolating the earnings of S&P Global, excluding the Mobility division, to derive the possible multiple expansion currently shadowed by the consolidated structure. As per the 2025 outlook, the Mobility division operates at an adjusted operating margin of ~39.5%-40% and contributes ~11.45% of consolidated revenue. Against that, the Ratings and Indices divisions operate at higher margins of ~65.5% and ~71.5%. The forward consolidated P/E ratio of ~29.6x penalizes the higher-quality segments with the lower-margin cyclicality.

To derive the price target, I apply the management’s reaffirmed medium-term target of double-digit adjusted diluted EPS growth and project a conservative 15% growth rate (below the historical 17% and a bit above the 13.7% to be realized in FY2025) onto the 2025 EPS estimate/guidance ($16.15 post-separation, removing 9.5% of bottom-line from Mobility on a Q3 basis), yielding a FY2029 estimate of $28.25. After the separation of Mobility in 2026, the remaining New SPGI will realize a structural margin uplift (enterprise-wide) and a concentration of recurring proprietary revenue (95%+). This superior quality profile can be linked to a forward premium valuation multiple through a re-rating to at least 30.75x (~SPGI 5Y forward average) under mean reversion. Applying a 30.75x multiple to the 2029 earnings leads to a price target of ~$868.7 (>60% upside).

SPGI Stock Alpha Protocol For Pre- and Post-Q4 2025 Earnings

SPGI bulls should accumulate long-positions pre-Q4 2025 earnings. S&P Global’s upward revision of 2025 guidance in October and projection of billed issuance growth in the mid-to-high teens for Q4 mean high momentum that likely outpaces seasonal slowing. The announced $2.5 billion share repurchase program for Q4 works as immediate downside support for the SPGI stock price ahead of Q4 earnings. For post-Q4 2025 Earnings, under the long-side strategy, I will monitor the 2026 outlook. Mainly, I will check the organic revenue guidance for Market Intelligence to confirm it is in line with the 6%-8% mid-term organic division target and affirmation for my Standardization Alpha thesis. If SPGI stock experiences volatility due to transaction costs related to the With Intelligence integration/Mobility spin-off preparations, it should be viewed as a buying point, as the core "Ratings" issuance forecast remains stable against the $8.2 trillion maturity wall through 2028.

_

All in all, S&P Global is a strong buy now, as I see it at the core of restructuring the financial data market. My reason for this rating is S&P Global's edging out process on private market infrastructure that converts discretionary data spend into non-discretionary requirements for clients (regulatory/fiduciary). For the long term, I will monitor two specific forward elements. One is the Recurring Revenue % within Market Intelligence (currently 96%) to detect any degradation from AI-based API consumption. The second one is the Billed Issuance volume in the high-yield segment to cross-check that the refinancing wall is converting into transaction revenue as projected.

data.title: United Natural Foods: This Meal Is Getting Tastier
data.description:
The last several months have been a really good time to be a shareholder of United Natural Foods (UNFI). Shares of the company have shot up 33.9% since I called it a ‘buy’ in March of last year. In that same time, the S&P 500 is up only 25.1%. This move higher is in response to improvements that the company has exhibited, specifically when it comes to the bottom line. On top of this, it seems as though the market is taking the recent traction as evidence that management can deliver on their promises for the next few years. Based on my own assessment, shares of the company are attractively priced. And if the company can achieve the kind of growth it is forecasting, the annualized upside could be massive. In light of all of this, I firmly believe that maintaining it as a very solid ‘buy’ is appropriate. And honestly, eventually upgrading it could make sense.

Investors have had a good meal

Given where we are in early 2026, I don't think a deep dive into the last several years for United Natural Foods would be all that relevant. But just so that we know where we are, in the chart above, we can see financial results for the 2023 through 2025 fiscal years for the company. During that time, revenue for the business improved. The bottom line results have been all over the map. Most noteworthy is the fact that net profits and most cash flow metrics have indeed worsened. But when you look at the more recent data provided by management, which covers the first quarter of the 2026 fiscal year, things look different.

In the chart above, you can see precisely what I mean. During that quarter, revenue came in at $7.84 billion. That is unfortunately down from the $7.87 billion the company reported a year earlier. Normally, I would dislike any drop in revenue. But we do need to talk about why sales declined. According to management, this drop in revenue was driven almost entirely by the company’s Conventional segment. This focuses on the wholesale distribution of conventional grocery and non-food products and services that the company has, which also includes some of its own conventional owned brands.

Because of how new the current arrangement of operating segments is, we don't really have reliable results dating back before 2024. But in the chart above, you can see how this segment performed for the last two completed fiscal years and for the first quarter of 2026 compared to the same time in 2025. Management has become less and less focused on these operations, with revenue dropping and profitability coming in mixed. Instead, the firm's efforts have been on something else. And that happens to be its Natural segment. This involves the wholesale distribution of natural, organic, and specialty grocery and non-food products and services, which includes some of its own owned brands, such as natural and organic snack food.

This is a large market opportunity. As of 2024, split between the conventional and natural market segments, the overall size of the market that the company was targeting was about $90 billion. $60 billion of this is the natural space, which compares to the $30 billion involving conventional offerings. And the disparity in size associated with these two market segments is likely to continue to get larger. After all, management says that the conventional market has been flat. But the natural market is growing. Considering the health consciousness of younger generations, this makes a great deal of sense. And management expects that, by 2029, the firm will be able to tap into various opportunities in what it believes is, combined, a market growing at a low single digit rate.

One particularly interesting angle here is the potential for the company to invest even more into private brands under the natural category. When it comes to the brands that it owns, its penetration in the conventional market is four times larger than it is in the natural market. Private brands are excellent because they tend to have greater margins and provide the firms that own them greater flexibility compared to brands owned by other customers. So this should be a big source of expansion over the next few years if everything goes according to plan.

It is worth noting that while the Natural and Conventional segments have experienced very different results over the last couple of years, the Retail segment of the company has unfortunately followed the Conventional segment. This has been largely intentional, though. The chart above shows financial performance for this segment for the 2024 and 2025 fiscal years, as well as for the first quarter of 2026 compared to the same time in 2025. Revenue dropped, and bottom line results worsened. But this seems to stem from the fact that the number of locations that the company has in operation did contract from 76 to 70. So again, this is evidence that management is pushing all of their efforts toward their Natural segment.

When management announced results for the first quarter of 2026, they said that revenue for the 2026 fiscal year in its entirety would come in at between $31.6 billion and $32 billion. At the midpoint, that would be around what the company achieved for 2025. However, because of the changes that management is making, and improvements that it has achieved on the bottom line, which can be seen in the chart that shows financial performance for the first quarter of 2026 compared to the same time in 2025, overall EBITDA is expected to climb from $552 million last year to between $630 million and $700 million this year. This would likely translate to an adjusted operating cash flow of about $353 million.

By continuing to make a push that it is in the direction of not only its Natural segment, but also toward private label brands, the company is targeting low single digit sales growth for the 2025 through 2028 fiscal years. It's also hoping to boost profitability, not only through organic growth, but also by making productivity improvements and focusing on changes to its supply chain. If everything goes according to plan, then by 2028, EBITDA should come in at around $800 million. That would translate to an adjusted operating cash flow of about $425 million at that point.

Using these estimates, we can see in the chart above how shares of the company are priced. They are especially appealing on a forward basis for 2028. In the chart below, I then compared it to five other similar firms. What I found was that, on a price-to-operating cash flow basis, only one of the five firms was cheaper than it is. Meanwhile, on an EV-to-EBITDA basis, it ended up being the cheapest of the group. So this alone justifies potential upside. And that would be in the form of multiple expansions. However, even if we assume that the company continues to trade at a discount to the competition, upside could be quite material from here. For instance, if the company continues to trade at the same multiple as it's at and achieves its targets for 2028, this would imply upside, using the price-to-adjusted operating cash flow approach, of 29.1% per annum. And using the EV to EBITDA approach, upside would be around 24.7% annually. Considering that the S&P 500 typically averages between 10% and 11% per year, this provides a lot of wiggle room for investors. And it certainly justifies, in my view, a more bullish stance.

Takeaway

Taken altogether, it is my belief that the market continues to underestimate United Natural Foods. It is an interesting company that is doing a solid job of making improvements. It's growing in key areas, and shares are trading on the cheap on both an absolute basis and relative to similar firms. Given this combination of factors, I feel very comfortable with my ‘buy’ rating on it.

data.title: 2 Big Fat Yields I Think You'll Like
data.description:
Introduction

A few days ago, I wrote an article on Business Development Companies (“BDCs”). I used it to explain that while I am not a big fan of the credit industry, in general, there’s some great value to be bought.

I discussed challenges like potentially lower variable rates for BDC deals, as the Fed has been cutting rates since the end of 2024. As this impacts the SOFR rate, the BDC industry, which mainly relies on variable debt deals to middle-market companies, is seeing some income headwinds.

If we assume most BDCs charge SOFR (which used to be LIBOR in the past) plus 5% to 6% on a deal, you can imagine how big a deal the SOFR jump from 0% in 2021 to more than 5% in 2024 was. Suddenly, many of these companies could charge more than 10% on a deal.

This caused many investors to jump into this industry, as I explained in my BDC article. Unfortunately, because of two problems, BDCs have lost some of their appeal:

  • Interest rates came down, which caused some BDCs to cut their dividends.
  • While GDP growth remained strong, general economic weakness in some cyclical areas in recent years has caused some BDCs to deal with higher default risks. This included the bankruptcy of First Brands. Also, I have made the case that because of AI disruption, aggressive investments in software loans over the past 1-2 decades may not be as safe as one might have expected, which deserves a lot more scrutiny (I think).

Generally speaking, however, private credit remains healthy, as portfolio companies see steady EBITDA growth, sustainable debt multiples, and healthy loan-to-value ratios.

Nonetheless, if there’s one thing I made clear, it’s that I don’t care much for BDCs at a discount, or at least, it’s not a determining factor. As much as I like to buy a good deal on value, my opinion is that in the lending business, some companies deserve to trade at a premium or a discount. Paying for a premium often pays off through lower financial risks and a more sustainable dividend.

I happily pay a premium and accept a “lower” yield if that means my downside risk is slightly lower and the dividend is better protected. Painting with a broad brush, the choice between a risky 13% yield and a safer 9% yield is easy.

With that said, the comment section (as always) was extremely interesting, as many seemed to agree with my thesis that aiming for a good discount may not be the best way to approach BDCs.

That also means it’s time for me to make good on a promise, which is to shed some light on two companies I have never given the attention they deserve. These are Fidus Investment (FDUS) and Trinity Capital (TRIN), two BDCs yielding 10% and 12%, respectively.

In the second part of this article, I’ll shed light on them and explain why I have put them on my high-yield watchlist in case I ever decide I’m going to let my money work for me in retirement.

So, let’s get to it!

Fidus And Trinity, Two Very Special Income Stocks

Unlike Trinity, which is an internally-managed BDC, Fidus is an externally-managed BDC. That goes for most companies that are basically lending vehicles that pay fees to an asset manager.

What makes Fidus unique is that it has a focus on the lower middle market with more willingness to take actual equity positions (these can be warrants or direct investments), along with the usual debt investments that are common in this industry.

Essentially, it’s a bigger partner to many companies, as it not only makes money from lending money, but also from equity investments (see below). I would say this gives them a lot more skin in the game, so to speak.

I like this approach a lot. I also like the focus on smaller companies with annual EBITDA of $5 million to $30 million. By focusing on smaller companies, Fidus is avoiding direct competition for deals with giants like Ares Capital Corp. (ARCC) and some of the bigger players in the industry.

Lower competition means more pricing power. After all, if you’re a borrower who can choose from multiple offers, you’re in a much better spot to dictate the terms. Smaller companies cannot do that.

Needless to say, Fidus isn’t a loan shark that exploits the absence of major competition in some deals. They build long-term relationships with borrowers (including through equity, as we just discussed). That has benefits like growing alongside each other and being in a better spot to assess credit risk. For example, I am much more likely to lend $1,000 to one of my friends than someone I just met, even if they may be in a better financial spot.

It also explains why less than 3.0% of all deals they scan end up in their portfolio, as they have a very extensive screening process that filters out anything that may not come with a great risk/reward. After all, as I often say, the lending business is tough. Lending capital at elevated rates is the easiest thing in the world. Getting it back is where it gets tough.

In this industry, investing is all about finding the players that best balance risks and rewards.

As of 3Q25, it had a portfolio with a fair value of $1.2 billion. This covered 92 portfolio companies with an average net leverage ratio of 5.5x and interest coverage of 3.1x. Most of its deals were first lien debt deals, with a focus on information technology and business sciences. That doesn’t come as a surprise, as tech has been a favorite among BDCs for years.

Moreover, total equity investments accounted for 12.0% of the portfolio at fair value. Long-term, it aims to invest 10% of new capital in equity. I like that, as I already mentioned, as it better aligns interest and provides FDUS with more upside if its portfolio companies succeed. It may also have something to do with the fact that I’m an “equity guy.”

With regard to its dividend, the company has a 100% total payout ratio, which is based on a $0.50 payout. That consists of a $0.43 base dividend (9% yield with an 86% payout) and a special dividend that brings this number to $0.50 (10% yield).

The dividend is also protected by a non-accrual rate of just 1.0% on the portfolio value.

Moreover, according to Raymond James data that the company brought up, between its IPO and 2Q25, it has been one of the five best-performing BDCs.

Then there’s Trinity, which is internally managed. While that is somewhat unique, it becomes even more unique if we incorporate the fact that this company invests a lot in things like venture debt and equipment financing for companies in the growth stage of their life cycle.

For example, it has term loans with companies like Whoop and Tilt, which are tech-like companies that require growth to scale their operations.

Equipment finance includes companies like Rocket Lab (RKLB). Unlike software companies, these players need capital for hard assets like factories, rockets, equipment, and so much more. They use these assets as collateral and often get on-demand credit from players like Trinity. When I was a corporate treasury intern at a major German chemical company, we obviously didn’t do BDCs, but I did see how this on-demand capital worked with credit lines.

These are just two examples of many. Essentially, Trinity looks for deals that are backed by venture companies and require growth funding. The quote below from their 3Q25 earnings call is key:

Our originations activity reflects consistent growth in all our verticals across the Trinity platform. It's a powerful flywheel fueled by our lead team of originators, and we own the pipeline. We do not depend on syndicated deals and have immaterial overlap with other BDCs, all of which give our investors access to a highly differentiated portfolio of investments through our 5 business verticals. All the while, we remain deeply committed to disciplined underwriting and credit performance, which are the bedrock of our long-term success. - TRIN 3Q25 Earnings Call

In other words, the company sources its own deals, has a wide platform for various solutions, and it doesn’t go in on deals with others. This gives it control and more influence.

And it gets better, as the company usually applies an interest rate floor, meaning falling rates have a limited impact:

I would like to touch on 2 noteworthy topics concerning the private credit space. First, let's talk about rate cuts. To date, rate cuts have had a limited impact on our business. Unlike most BDCs, the majority of our loans include interest rate floors at or near the original closing levels. This means that when rates decline, our income does not decline proportionately. Looking ahead, additional rate cuts are expected to have a muted impact on our returns, partially due to a majority of our portfolio having already hit their floor rates, which could drive some early repayments and the capturing of prepayment fees and restructuring fees. - TRIN 3Q25 Earnings Call

I really like this business, as it finds good deals that not only provide growth, but also can take on deals with an interest rate floor. Also, slightly less than 10% of its $2.0 billion portfolio consists of warrants, which adds upside potential and more alignment.

This platform also provides embedded growth options.

As of 3Q25, the company generated $75.6 million in total investment income. That’s 22% more than in 3Q24. It resulted in net investment income of $0.52 per share. That number implies a 102% dividend coverage. It has paid 23 consecutive quarters of “consistent dividends”. This means the dividend hasn’t been cut in nearly six years. Currently, it yields 12%.

And, just like Fidus, its non-accruals were just 1.0% of its fair value.

I have to say that in the double-digit income BDC space, TRIN has become my favorite.

Valuation-wise, TRIN trades at a 27% premium to its book value, while FDUS can be bought at book value. While I wish FDUS would come down a bit, as it usually trades close to a 15% premium, if I were in the market for BDC income today, it would not keep me from buying.

Now, onto the takeaway!

Takeaway

I am not the biggest fan of the lending business. I have said that many times. Too often, almost. However, I never said there’s no good value in these spaces.

Today, we discussed two companies I consider terrific investments.

On the one hand, we have Fidus, which is a more defensive lower-middle-market player with a strong focus on the equity component, and Trinity, a more aggressive growth player with a more unique contract structure and exposure to what I consider faster-growing VC-backed lenders. It also has more downside protection from falling rates due to its rate floors.

I believe both avoid the yield trap and offer income with a relatively attractive risk/reward.

Risks To My Thesis

I already briefly mentioned them in the intro, but the biggest risks are falling rates and deteriorating economic growth. One component lowers interest income (assuming limited rate floors), while the other component can cause non-accrual rates to rise and total loan demand to fall.

In general, investors need to be aware that the lending business is very cyclical. As much as I believe that FDUS and TRIN dividends are safe in this environment, a steep recession could cause dividend cuts.

data.title: Frontera Energy: Finally Some Recovery
data.description:
Frontera Energy (FECCF) announced the sale of the Colombia upstream business that will raise up to $400 million. Of that amount, there is a $25 million contingent payment. The company will keep the infrastructure business that it was going to spin off in an earlier announcement. The first article that I wrote on this company was rated a hold back then until I became alarmed as events unfolded. By the time the last article was written the stock price was down considerably from that initial (roughly) C$12 price back in 2023.

The current announced sale sent the stock soaring to recover a lot of the losses since 2023. But that is nothing to brag about. The fact of the matter is that this stock has not really done all that well. The fact that management also announced that the cash received will be largely returned to shareholders is a tacit admission that this management cannot find profitable alternatives for that cash.

It is also inconsistent with the mention of the Guyana assets that are now impaired. One of the big issues that resulted in the impairment was the financing of the costs of the discovery to potentially determine commercial levels of oil present and then the costs to produce it (and get it to market). So, it is interesting that the company raised $400 million through this sale and then plans to give the money to shareholders while pressing for a negotiation with the Guyana government on the lease takeover.

The other thing to consider is that there is still some debt left that management evidently considers reasonable. But as the stock price history indicates (since the first article in 2023), financial leverage has not worked to the advantage of the company.

In this industry, financial leverage not working is not all that unusual. Frankly, leverage works best when the cash flow is certain. But with oil and gas, the commodity prices are volatile and have low future visibility. Even though the remaining assets are a combination of infrastructure and some upstream assets, it would appear that really there should be no leverage.

This company should develop a far better record for shareholders before it considers debt in really any form.

2025

The company will soon report the fourth quarter and the full year for fiscal year 2025. Dominating the results will be the impairment taken as shown below:

Keep in mind that there is a substantial impairment to the Guyana business. Even though management intends to pursue its claims, the taking of an impairment on the assets pretty much tells investors that any pursuit is likely to be an uphill battle.

One of the reasons that the articles started with a hold but a few months later moved to strong sell was that the company was going against what I thought was traditional industry practice for these offshore leases.

Companies like Meren Energy (MRNFF) and Murphy Oil (MUR) routinely handle the questions that caused the impairment here without really any trouble. As a result, these two companies continue to participate in the offshore industry without any question of their ability to finance their share of whatever joint venture is involved (or single venture). This company ran into issues almost from the start of the lease. To me, it looks like the company simply ran out of time to convince the Guyana government that it had a handle on the situation.

But then again, let us see how the future unfolds. I have seen some interesting things in my day. So, nothing would surprise me at all.

The fourth quarter should update investors on the remaining businesses. But more importantly, the company needs to avoid a repeat of the current fiscal year. Now rebuilding a satisfactory track record will not happen overnight. But that is what needs to happen.

As it is, I firmly believe that the sale to GeoPark (GPRK) is probably a very good thing, as GeoPark is a company that has in the past experienced considerable success in South America. Now, as to whether these assets will be successful under this new management, is something to watch and see what happens.

Summary

The recent sale recoups a lot of the stock price decline since I first began to follow the company. Now admittedly, the stock price does show a considerable gain over the price of the article at the time it was published for the last several articles. But since I began following the company, even after today's run-up, it is still below my first article on this company in 2023.

To me, this means there was a trading opportunity for those smart enough to catch the trade and make some money off of it. But I write for buy and hold investors. To me, there are much better opportunities than what was happening here. All the proof I needed for that assertion came in the form of a big impairment charge.

Many times, my strong sell happens to mean there are better risk-reward opportunities than what is presented here for buy and hold (with a tendency to forget) investors. It does not mean, like in this case, that management will not have an announcement that causes the stock price to run up for one reason or another.

I happen to like asymmetric returns while avoiding big losses. I think that this management is far too aggressive for my framework of avoiding big losses. More importantly, that aggressiveness is not showing results that offset the risk incurred (in any way I have ever learned).

So, while a stock like this can have its successes from time to time, this is not how I prefer to invest. For me, this remains a strong sell. I personally get out the popcorn and the soda while watching from the sidelines because I prefer much more conservative managements.

Another choice for Colombia for me would be Parex (PARXF). This company is traditionally very low debt or no debt with a dividend yield of more than 7%. Given the very strong balance sheet, that dividend can be considered very defensible. That dividend alone is what most investors report for a long-term annual return (or at least close to it). That is the kind of safety I like.

Risks

Management could effectively run the remaining assets and begin an attractive growth record from this point. It has happened in the past. But to me, the risk of having faith in management to do that is outweighed by the potential risk of more future losses after the payment and the market valuation of the remaining businesses.

Note that on Monday, February 2, 2026, the stock could be trading below the value of the cash payment and the remaining business enough that some may be tempted to invest and wait for that payment. I really have no problems with such a strategy as long as an investor knows what they are doing in circumstances like this (and realizes there is always a chance that the deal comes undone).

But for the long term, I see much better opportunities elsewhere. The United States stock market seems to fear a future recession. Such an event could wreak havoc with a company like this. That would especially be true if the recession is worse than expected and/or lasts longer than expected.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: This Time, A Weaker Dollar Is Part Of The Plan
data.description:
The Monetary Counterpart To Geo-Capex

After Davos, we talked about geo-capex as the best conceptual frame that explains the current macroeconomic environment and that sets the tone for the next decade. We discussed the duplication of supply chains and infrastructure at the expense of pursuing efficiency above all. This has some implications that could help us understand better what is also happening to the dollar (DXY) and gold (GLD). I see, in fact, that the industrial policy we have been discussing and covering has some links with the current dollar debasement.

In fact, we are seeing a sharp repricing of the dollar, which challenges its role as a reserve currency. However, we also heard that the American President recently said that "the value of the dollar is great", perhaps implying that what is happening was somewhat anticipated and even welcomed.

Let's then take a moment to consider a few things together.

We already saw that the market is pricing in new geopolitical risk with the U.S.; in addition, we also considered that big regional investments in AI, energy, strategic infrastructure, manufacturing reshoring, and similar require huge private and public resources. I invite you to read Professor Damodaran's blog which often discusses these topics and gives us a highly structured academic point of view. This is making many question whether this massive cycle is adhering to financial orthodoxy or if something is a bit off. In other words, some investors are starting to ask what ROI should be expected. Let's leave this aside for a moment. What matters more is that a weak dollar doesn't appear as an accident. It is, in fact, the true monetary flipside of the geo-capex process we have been describing.

The New System

In the 19th century, America relied on tariffs and protection, and many are seeing that the 21st century, especially with the new administration, is going in that direction once again. Surely, trade policy has been recently viewed as a way to reduce the trade deficit and boost the reshoring process to create a new manufacturing backbone in the U.S., attracting billions of foreign investments. For now, we are seeing things turn out a bit differently than what many economists predicted. Inflation seems to be stable, hovering around 3%. However, we recently learned that the trade deficit in November 2025 increased by 94.6% in November, reaching $56.8B.

Fiscal policy is in expansion mode. The OBBBA and the Trump accounts represent two examples that require new debt (possibly at low interest rates). This is why many government-sponsored enterprises (GSEs) are buying mortgage-backed securities, which is a way to create, in a certain way, a QE "under disguise" that pushes up the available money supply. In other words, the boundary between fiscal policy and monetary support becomes a bit more blurred, even though no official QE has been announced.

The consequence is that the value of the dollar goes down, and this seems to be at least tolerated by the President.

Now, while a weak dollar might weaken currency prestige abroad, I think the recent actions in Iran and Venezuela have more than compensated for this possible loss by reaffirming at least the American military dominance (which, as textbooks teach, can't be sustained if the underlying economy isn't strong). At the same time, a weaker dollar is very useful domestically. In fact, defense spending, investing in energy security, reshoring strategic supply chains, building AI infrastructure all require big private investments and/or fiscal deficits. When a currency is overvalued, this becomes harder to achieve.

I want, therefore, to say that geo-capex needs a weaker dollar because exporters see the cost of inflation being offset, while domestic businesses indirectly receive a sort of subsidy. This means that import prices are burdened, while domestic wages are not. This might help the U.S. labor market more than we would have thought a year ago.

Dollar Weakness: Implications For Commodities, Bonds, And Equities

Usually, when the dollar is weak, the asset classes that outperform are non-U.S. equities, or U.S. companies whose international revenues represent a big part of their total revenues. But a weaker dollar is also a tailwind for most commodities. Oil, in particular, can often rise; metals do too, and agricultural products also benefit. Let's then look at metals. Gold has a strong inverse correlation with the dollar. But what we have recently seen is unique. Gold and silver are seeing immense inflows of cash. We have talked about central banks selling U.S. Treasuries and buying gold. We said that this is because gold and silver don't carry any political risk attached to them. They are "politically de-risked", as once U.S. Treasuries were considered to be.

A very interesting article recently published on the "Council on Foreign Relations" recently explained that all these things that we are seeing could be part of a "Mar-a-Lago Accord", whose main goal would be to see the dollar lose value. In the meantime, it seems that both the U.S. and the Japanese governments would welcome a stronger yen, which would make American exports to Japan easier. At the same time, a weaker dollar is also a hidden "tax" for foreign investors, who would see their returns reduced as their home currency gains value against their dollar-denominated assets. This is also already true when we hear that big pension funds are selling US bonds.

This creates an environment where, once again (it is one of the leitmotifs I have been repeating), hard assets win. This time, metals are included, even though, technically speaking, they are not cash-generating holdings. We could then expand our view and say that hard money is the counterpart of the bull case on hardware and infrastructure. It shows that investors need scarce assets (and metals are).

Finally, an environment where geo-capex goes along with a debasement trend in the dollar obviously creates a negative landscape for long-duration nominal assets.

I wanted to show that geo-capex has a monetary flipside. This means that I don't view a weak dollar as just a short-term FX signal. It could be part of a structural trend that is deeper than what we might think. It also shows once again that hard and material assets are relevant and that monetary neutrality is not a true part of our current world. Let me explain this. Geo-capex will require governments to have access to cheap money. This means that neutral policies don't make sense anymore, as they made sense in the past decade. This also explains why we are seeing a clash between the U.S. President and the Fed on interest rates. While a president can't lower interest rates, he can work for currency devaluation, which is accepted in a trade-off where domestic goals are more easily achieved in exchange. Money now is more directional compared to just a few years ago. And this also leads to one final idea: cash is not as risk-free as we thought.

Actionable Investing Ideas

I will keep pounding the table on equities with exposure to geo-capex. I like Prysmian (PRYMY), in particular, because it is the indisputable leader in high-voltage cables and has the best fleet in the world to lay undersea cables. But I would also consider that Big Tech stocks involved in the AI buildout and in the cloud will eventually be assessed as strategic assets. A company such as Caterpillar (CAT), although expensive, also plays a meaningful role today that goes way beyond being an excavator manufacturer. Eaton (ETN), on the other hand, could offer a better entry point while giving us exposure to grids. Moreover, we could consider U.S. homebuilders such as D.R. Horton (DHI). After all, if GSEs are buying mortgage-backed securities, this could be a tailwind for the housing market. Finally, considering that agricultural commodities could benefit, I would start considering companies such as Deere (DE) or AGCO Corporation (AGCO), but also the two Canadian Class 1 railroads, which are highly exposed to grains.

Conclusion

Given that we are talking about a strategic devaluation that could be converging with the geo-capex trend, three main investing baskets become highly important: infrastructure sovereignty plays, hard money, and export-sensitive manufacturers. Overall, I think these trends will fuel the U.S. economy for some time, and this is why I keep a bullish view on the market. The environment, however, won't be the same for everyone. There will be stocks that will suffer, especially among importers and consumer discretionary.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: What Moved Markets This Week
data.description:
Listen on the go! A daily podcast of Wall Street Breakfast will be available by 8:00 a.m. on Seeking Alpha, iTunes, Spotify.

Seeking Alpha News Quiz

Up for a challenge? Test your knowledge on the biggest events in the investing world over the past week. Take the latest Seeking Alpha News Quiz and see how you stack up against the competition.

Seeking Alpha's Calls Of The Week

Call It A Dip Or Correction… Either Way I'm Buying Netflix (NFLX).

Upgrading Blackstone (BX) As Trump's Home Buying Ban Is Noise.

Seritage Growth (SRG) Reduces Term Loan Debt After Aventura Sale.

Sun Communities' (SUI) MH, RV Portfolio Grows After Exiting Marinas.

Moving To Sidelines on Apple (AAPL) - iPhone 17 Triggers Supercycle.

Downgrading Centene (CNC) To Hold On Timing, Not Fundamentals.

ASGI Has Solid Portfolio, Performance And Yield, But Terrible Price.

CAVA: Sell This Rebound Ahead Of Potentially Disappointing Results.

Reveiwing LVMH's (LVMHF) Earnings - Consumer Habits Are Changing.

Turning Bearish On VGK As Europe Set To Face Another Energy Crisis.


Wall Street ended the trading week in the red as most of the Magnificent Seven stocks reporting earnings did not lift sentiments, contributing to tech trade sell-off, as the Federal Reserve hit a pause in its interest rate cutting cycle, and precious metals suffered a rollercoaster of highs before turning lower.

The S&P 500 (SP500), nonetheless, managed to cross the 7,000 level for the first time on Tuesday before retracing back. The main indexes then followed a rocky path downhill.

Gold (XAUUSD:CUR) reached $5,600 mid-week, while silver (XAGUSD:CUR) topped $118, before coming down, while the U.S. dollar (DXY) climbed on Friday. Mining names also suffered as U.S. President Trump nominated Kevin Warsh to become the Fed chair.

This week on earnings, some of the Magnificent Seven names released their reports, failing to raise the main averages. Microsoft (MSFT) endured one of its worst sell-off days even after a positive result. Meta (META) shares also declined despite a beat on earnings.

For the week, the S&P (SP500) added +0.3%, while the tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) dipped -0.2%, and the blue-chip Dow (DJI) fell -0.4%. Read a preview of next week's major events in Seeking Alpha's Catalyst Watch.
